desc: Essaouira is the kind of Atlantic break that works even when the beach is not the whole point: cannon-lined ramparts, blue boats and seafood by the port, a UNESCO medina that is calmer than Marrakech, and enough wind for kitesurfing, surfing and long salty walks. The city is small and easy to use, but its airport is thinner than travellers often assume, so routing and season matter as much as the weather.

todo.name: Medina ramparts
todo.desc: Walk the Skala walls for cannon-lined Atlantic views and blue-boat harbour scenes.

todo.name: Port seafood stalls
todo.desc: Build lunch around grilled fish near the harbour when the daily catch is moving through.

todo.name: Kitesurf or windsurf lesson
todo.desc: Use Essaouira's reliable wind for a lesson, especially if pure sunbathing is not the goal.

todo.name: Gnaoua music trail
todo.desc: Look for live music venues or festival dates tied to the city's Gnaoua identity.

wifi.note: Usually reliable in riads, cafes and coworking-friendly stays inside or just outside the medina, but thicker walls and beach-club day setups can make mobile data a helpful backup.

prac.fltNote: Essaouira-Mogador is a small airport about 16 km from town. In current schedules the London nonstop is tied to Stansted rather than LGW or LCY, so destination-level direct flags from those UI origins stay false. Marrakech is still the stronger fallback gateway when Essaouira frequencies are thin or seasonal, with a road transfer of roughly 2.5 to 3.5 hours depending on traffic and stops.
prac.lang: Arabic and Amazigh; French is widely useful and English varies
prac.visa: British citizens can normally visit Morocco visa-free for up to 90 days for tourism, but should still check passport-validity requirements and any carrier-specific document checks before travel.
alerts: Wind is excellent for boards but can frustrate beach lounging.
Festival periods sell out well ahead.
Evenings can feel cool because of Atlantic exposure.

sweet: April to June and September to October are the best balance for medina wandering, seafood lunches and wind-sport conditions without the busier midsummer squeeze.
